column2: Ask Miyechka
We've arrived
— Every living creature has a right to shelter, — said Miyechka at the wheel.
— To a shelter overgrown with bushes, — her sister Lilichka retorted.
While they were silent, they were absolutely different: one had wavy hair, the other — straight, like a Chinese woman's.
Miyechka's cheeks always had a faintly peachy tint, while Lilichka was pale, as if she had spent her whole life in a concrete box that the sun never reaches.
But when the sisters began to speak, they somehow became alike.
With every word their eyes, lips, ears, movements merged more and more, until the listener would close his eyes, shake his head, and say: 'Which of you is speaking?'.
Then they would exchange glances, stay silent for a moment, and again become different — like day and night.
They always came to the bush-overgrown refuge for losers whenever they simply could no longer do without it.
It took no tragedies for that — sometimes it just seemed as if everything were in a fog and the road ahead could not be seen.
Then the time for the shelter would come.

The refuge was a house with enormous, disproportionate windows, a worn floor and a rotted terrace, all overgrown with bushes, raspberry, blackberry, hops, tall poplars, birches, wild plums, apples and pears.
It was in those bushes that they had grown up, lost the battle to the thickets and undergrowth, and from there they had left.
It stood like a reproach to the past, that things there would never be as they were before.

Never again would they all sleep three to a bed, never would the men say 'I'll lie outside' and lie down on the wooden planks beneath the trees, shaking off all night long the chance bats, cats, and insects with their endless number of legs.
All these people had long since scattered, died, gotten lost, and they were recalled only by assorted junk named after them.
'Hand me the Goncharov vase,' the grandmother would say.
No one in the house knew or remembered that Goncharov, but the vase bore his name.

Where the house stood there had once been a cottage, and then relentless time ground it down, leaving a caved-in roof, a shed sunk chest-deep into the ground, and a touching little ladder by the small window for the poultry.
No bird was fated to sit on that ladder any longer, except the occasional yellow orioles.
Once, a man had brought the grandmother to this place, taking her down from a fifth floor in the city center, away from her parents' ficuses, the library, and the gold-embroidered tablecloth.
— Is this the paradise you told me about?
— asked Teodora, still young then, looking at the old shed and at the remains of the ruined cottage, which looked as if it had been wracked by convulsions.
— This is it, — she heard in reply, and understood that she loved that voice so much that no ficuses were needed by her at all.
Decades passed before the traces of the old cottage vanished, and in its place a house grew up and grew old together with Teodora.
Here children grew up, from here they left, grandchildren grew up, here great-grandchildren played.

Now Teodora, approaching her hundredth year, sat on the rotted terrace, having made her way along the path all overgrown with blackberry, and waited for her refugee girls, who were heading toward her into the bushes.
At first they fled to the refuge over existential anguish about dead parrots and turtles.
Then over career crises, loves, children.
At the refuge Lilichka rocked her sons in those times when, under ordinary conditions, they would drive her to madness.
In the shelter for losers the children suddenly grew calm and simply crawled through the blackberry thickets, scratching their tender, soft little bellies.
Here Miyechka decided where to move next.
In the shelter ideas did not come into her head, but instead came the awareness that someday they would after all.
Here one could even come to terms with the absence of ideas, while hacking at those impassable thickets.
— It's frightening to imagine what people do who have no house in the wild raspberry, — said Lilichka.

She had brought faintly pink open-toed shoes for Miyechka to put on at once — the shoes had lain in the attic for some forty years; the grandmother had bought them once in Vilnius, but the size was not hers, so she had never worn them, and now did not know that their time had come.
Lilichka had found them while tidying the attic in a fit of nerves.
Who could have known that forty years ago the grandmother had bought shoes of exactly the size that the four feet of her two future granddaughters would be.
— You'll step out of the car now, and grandmother will fall over, — said Lilichka, studying her sister's dress.
She was officially beautiful.
She wore dark glasses, a tank top with nothing underneath it, and jeans with holes.
— And to you she'll say, — replied Miyechka, — why can't you dress like your sister?
— But she doesn't know that the rest of the time you wear that dress of yours, the color and cut of a potato sack, — said Lilichka, flashing her glasses into the rearview mirror.
— But it's short, at least.
— All the more so for being short.
Then they stopped at a gas station.

The first hot June sun was shining, somewhere out there under the apple trees the wild strawberries of their childhood were ripening, and Miyechka pulled her suitcase from the trunk and took out of it a sky-blue sundress with little white flowers.
— Oh!
— said Lilichka.
— Put it on!
— Miyechka smiled.
Lilichka opened the back door of the car, quickly threw off her tank top, and the sundress slid in a light wave from her shoulders down past her knees.
— Grandmother will fall over for sure, — said Lilichka.
For the first time in eight years the situation was such that rehabilitation was needed for the whole summer.
They had sent out letters to husbands, boyfriends, ex-husbands, and everyone concerned.
And they drew up a schedule for Lilichka's children.
— Do you remember, — said Miyechka, with one hand searching for a radio station and with the other adjusting the sleeve of her dress, — summer used to be endless, didn't it?
Lilichka just nodded, with one hand holding the wheel in Miyechka's place.
With her other hand she was rubbing an apple against her own knee, on which the just-gifted sundress lay in a light blue wave.
They always did it that way — when one of them set about fixing her straps, the other automatically took the wheel into her own hands.
They arrived already toward evening.
— God, couldn't you have dressed like this your whole life!
— the grandmother threw up her hands.
She had already been waiting for them.
— Granny, you've wasted twenty years!
— Lilichka laughed, straightening out the sundress crumpled from the journey.
— If you'd dressed like this all your life, maybe you'd have married a fourth time too, — said Miyechka, and quickly fled into the house before her sister could catch up.
Lilichka married spontaneously, but between marriages she always took breaks, went off somewhere far away, trying to put her thoughts in order.

She planned to take breaks lasting years, or never to tie herself down in any relationship at all again, but she was at the same time so criminally beautiful and so criminally helpless in her ascetic travels that she always came back with new love stories.
Everyone urgently wanted to marry her off, no one hesitated.
And that could not but be attractive.
In the evening the three of them sat by the garden.
Lilichka and Miyechka spread their great-grandmother's cotton-wool quilt, red as a communist flag, on the concrete steps and sat leaning against each other.
For the grandmother they brought a chair and set it across from themselves.
She could no longer sit with them on the steps.
— And what have you all come for?
— asked the grandmother.
Her question could in no way offend, because both sisters knew that it was a question born of worry.
— A mountain pass, Granny, — said Lilichka.
— And will we be crossing over for long?
— Until summer ends.
— So be it.

They sat like that until the mosquitoes had stopped paying any attention to the spritzing of the sprays, until the willows were almost no longer visible in the dusk, until hedgehogs began to crawl underfoot, and the frogs began to sing in the river.
For the night they left the window into the garden ajar, found nightgowns with lace sleeves, frills below the knees, and buttons on the chest.
The ones with buttons on the chest Lilichka could not wear, so she went and cut off every last button and neatly put them into a little vase on the windowsill.
The moon was barely shining, the hedgehogs rustled, and it was almost unnoticeable that no one had lived here for half a year.
— Li, — said Miyechka, — tell me.
— I don't know what to do, — said Lilichka, lying in the darkness.
— Will you warm my feet for me?
— All right.

Lilichka pressed her feet, cold as a little frog's, against her sister and told her how nothing was working out, how she did not want to move to Australia, how it seemed to her that it was not the only possible place to live, even though the world was growing closer, and even if Australia won't separate you and me, Miyechka, it will certainly separate our children.
— My children, — she clarified.
— And yours, who are yet to come.

— But I have no need at all for these children just yet, — said Miyechka, but both of them knew that this was an absolute lie, and that back at twenty she would sometimes walk down the street leading an imaginary three-year-old girl by the hand.
She had been taking her along spontaneously for almost a decade now.
— Li, — said Miyechka, — there are spiders and scorpions there.
— And kangaroos.
— Kangaroos look from above like men.
— And from below — like big-bottomed, tailed women.
What will I do there, Solomiya?
— asked Lilichka, raising herself up on her elbows.
— Have you seen a map of Australia?
Have you seen how much land there is there?
— I have.
— Maybe you'll become a farmer.

They moved closer to each other, put on a film about Australia, and until late at night watched how some handsome, tanned man drove through the desert in a jeep and slept on its roof, trying to photograph the stars on his phone.

The grandmother, for her part, in the morning, having fried up a skillet of eggs in lard, which Lilichka could not eat, so she made herself sandwiches, said that one must obey one's husband, and if it's Australia, then it's Australia.
And then she glanced at Lilichka, so thin, so unfit for some far-off Australia, and said:
— Maybe he'll change his mind?
— Do you mean to say that one just has to wait and see whether he'll change his mind?
— Miyechka cut in.
— Well, no, — the grandmother replied, — you have to, well, you know, give him a little push.
— Go on, then, — said Lilichka.
— Teach me some manipulation.
— If you haven't learned it by now, then you never will, — said Solomiya.
— And have you learned it, Miyechka?
— the grandmother tossed at Solomiya.
— It's an inborn gift, — she retorted, glancing quickly at her sister.
As if to say, shh, don't you dare lay it all out for grandmother right now.
